benign: Not cancer. Benign growths do not spread to other parts of the body.
malignant: Cancerous. Malignant cells can grow into nearby tissue and may spread elsewhere in the body.

carcinoma.definition: A cancer that starts in the cells lining organs or skin. It is the most common type of cancer.
carcinoma.short: A cancer that starts in the skin or the lining of organs.

invasive ductal carcinoma.definition: The most common type of breast cancer. It starts in a milk duct and has grown beyond it into the surrounding breast tissue.
invasive ductal carcinoma.short: A common breast cancer that has spread beyond the milk ducts into nearby tissue.

basal cell carcinoma.definition: The most common type of skin cancer. It starts in the deepest layer of the skin and usually grows slowly without spreading.
basal cell carcinoma.short: A common, usually slow-growing skin cancer.

adenocarcinoma: A cancer that starts in gland cells — cells that make mucus or other fluids (common in colon, breast, lung, and prostate).
sarcoma: A cancer that starts in bone, muscle, fat, or other connective tissue.
lymphoma: A cancer that starts in the lymphatic system, part of the body's germ-fighting network.
melanoma: A cancer that starts in the pigment-making cells of the skin (melanocytes).
neoplasm: A general word for an abnormal growth of cells. A neoplasm can be benign (not cancer) or malignant (cancer).
tumor: A lump or mass of abnormal cells. A tumor can be benign (not cancer) or malignant (cancer).
lesion: A general word for an area of abnormal tissue. It does not automatically mean cancer.
biopsy: A procedure that removes a small piece of tissue so it can be examined under a microscope.
excision: Surgical removal of tissue. An 'excisional biopsy' removes the whole abnormal area.
resection: Surgery to remove part or all of an organ or tissue.
specimen: The piece of tissue or fluid that was removed from your body and sent to the lab.
histology: The study of tissue under a microscope. 'Histologic type' describes what kind of cells were seen.
cytology: The study of individual cells (rather than whole tissue) under a microscope.
pathologist: The doctor who examines tissue under a microscope and writes the pathology report.

margin.definition: The edge of the tissue that was removed. Doctors check whether abnormal cells reach this edge.
margin.short: The edge of the removed tissue.

margins: The edges of the tissue that was removed. Doctors check whether abnormal cells reach the edge.
clear margins: No cancer cells were seen at the edges of the removed tissue — a good sign that the abnormal area was fully removed.
negative margins: Same as clear margins: no cancer cells at the edge of what was removed.
positive margins: Cancer cells were found at the edge of the removed tissue, which may mean some abnormal cells were left behind. More treatment may be discussed.

in situ.definition: Latin for 'in place.' The abnormal cells are only in the layer where they started and have not grown into deeper tissue.
in situ.short: The abnormal cells have not spread beyond where they started.

invasive.definition: The abnormal cells have grown beyond the layer where they started into nearby tissue.
invasive.short: The cancer has grown into nearby tissue.

infiltrating: Another word for invasive — the cells are growing into surrounding tissue.
metastasis: The spread of cancer from where it started to another part of the body.
metastatic: Describes cancer that has spread from its original location to another part of the body.

differentiated.definition: How much the cancer cells look like normal cells. 'Well differentiated' cells look more normal and often grow more slowly; 'poorly differentiated' cells look very abnormal and may grow faster.
differentiated.short: How much the cancer cells resemble normal cells.

well differentiated: The cells look a lot like normal cells. These cancers often grow and spread more slowly (low grade).

moderately differentiated.definition: The cells look somewhat abnormal — in between low grade and high grade.
moderately differentiated.short: The cells look somewhat abnormal, in between low and high grade.

poorly differentiated: The cells look very different from normal cells. These cancers may grow and spread more quickly (high grade).
grade: A score for how abnormal the cells look under the microscope, which hints at how fast the cancer may grow. Lower grade usually means slower-growing.
stage: How far a cancer has grown or spread in the body. Staging usually combines the pathology report with scans and other tests.

dysplasia.definition: Cells that look abnormal but are not cancer. Depending on the degree (mild, moderate, severe), they may need monitoring or removal.
dysplasia.short: Abnormal-looking cells that are not cancer.

atypia: Cells that look slightly abnormal under the microscope. Atypia is not cancer by itself.
atypical: Not entirely normal in appearance. Atypical cells are watched closely but are not automatically cancer.
hyperplasia: An increased number of normal-looking cells. It is not cancer, though some types can raise future risk.
polyp: A growth that sticks out from the lining of an organ such as the colon. Most polyps are benign, but some types can turn into cancer over time.

adenoma.definition: A benign (non-cancerous) tumor that starts in gland cells. Some adenomas, like colon adenomas, are removed because they can become cancer later.
adenoma.short: A non-cancerous tumor that starts in gland cells.

cyst: A closed sac that may contain fluid or other material. Most cysts are benign.
necrosis: Dead tissue or cells. In tumors, it often means the tumor outgrew its blood supply.
inflammation: The body's immune response to injury or irritation; the tissue may be swollen or filled with immune cells.
chronic: Long-lasting or ongoing (as opposed to 'acute', which means sudden or short-term).
acute: Sudden or recent in onset (as opposed to 'chronic').
lymph node: A small, bean-shaped part of the immune system. Pathologists check lymph nodes to see whether cancer has spread into them.
lymph nodes: Small, bean-shaped parts of the immune system. Pathologists check them to see whether cancer has spread.
lymphovascular invasion: Cancer cells were seen inside small blood vessels or lymph channels, which can make spread more likely.
perineural invasion: Cancer cells were seen growing along or around nerves.
mitotic: Related to cell division. A high 'mitotic rate' means the cells are dividing quickly.
proliferation: Cell growth and division. A high proliferation rate means cells are multiplying quickly.
ki-67: A lab marker that shows what percentage of cells are actively dividing. Higher numbers mean faster-growing cells.
immunohistochemistry: A lab technique that uses special stains to identify proteins on cells, helping determine the exact type of tumor.
receptor: A protein on or in a cell that can receive signals. Some cancers are tested for receptors (like hormone receptors) to guide treatment.
estrogen receptor: A protein some breast cancer cells have. 'ER-positive' cancers may respond to hormone-blocking treatments.
progesterone receptor: A protein some breast cancer cells have. 'PR-positive' cancers may respond to hormone-blocking treatments.
her2: A protein that can make some cancer cells grow faster. HER2-positive cancers can be treated with targeted drugs.
carcinoma in situ: Very early cancer that is still only in the layer of cells where it began and has not spread deeper.

ductal.definition: Related to the ducts (small tubes) of an organ, such as milk ducts in the breast.
ductal.short: Related to the milk ducts in the breast.

lobular: Related to the lobules — the small glands, for example the milk-producing glands of the breast.
squamous: Describes flat cells that line surfaces like the skin, mouth, throat, and cervix.
basal cell: A type of cell in the deepest layer of the skin. Basal cell carcinoma is a common, usually slow-growing skin cancer.
gleason score: A grading system for prostate cancer. Lower scores (6) suggest slower-growing cancer; higher scores (8-10) suggest faster-growing cancer.
tnm: A staging system: T describes the tumor size, N whether lymph nodes are involved, and M whether it has spread (metastasized).
unremarkable: Normal — nothing unusual was seen. In medical reports, 'unremarkable' is good news.
consistent with: The findings match or support a particular diagnosis.
suspicious for: The findings suggest, but do not prove, a particular diagnosis. More testing may be needed.
rule out: The doctor wants testing to confirm that a certain condition is NOT present.
prognosis: The likely course or outcome of a disease.
etiology: The cause of a disease or condition.
bilateral: On both sides of the body (for example, both breasts or both lungs).
unilateral: On one side of the body only.
proximal: Closer to the center of the body or the beginning of a structure.
distal: Farther from the center of the body or the end of a structure.
anterior: Toward the front of the body.
posterior: Toward the back of the body.
calcification: Small deposits of calcium in tissue. Often benign, but certain patterns are checked more closely.
encapsulated: Surrounded by a capsule or covering. Encapsulated tumors are often easier to remove completely.
ulceration: A break or open sore in the surface of tissue.
granuloma: A small cluster of immune cells that forms in response to infection or irritation. Usually not cancer.
edema: Swelling caused by fluid in the tissue.
erythema: Redness of the skin or tissue.
stenosis: Narrowing of a passage or vessel in the body.
occult: Hidden or too small to be seen without a microscope or special tests.
remission: The signs and symptoms of the disease have decreased or disappeared.
